text: The surgeon performs a laparoscopic total gastrectomy with DII lymphadenectomy in an obese patient, utilizing intracorporeal anastomosis techniques.
The video shows a fully differentiated adenocarcinoma located in the gastric incisory angularis, highlighting the tumor's position within the stomach anatomy.
Four trocars are positioned in the abdominal wall, along with a liver retractor to elevate the liver and provide clear access to the surgical field.
The surgeon begins the dissection by dividing the gastric column omentum, mobilizing the greater curvature of the stomach to access the lesser sac.
The surgeon removes the remaining omentum at the conclusion of the gastrectomy procedure, completing the mobilization of the greater curvature of the stomach.
The surgeon performs lymphadenectomy of stations IV-D and IV-SB, carefully excising lymphatic tissue from these specific anatomical regions during the laparoscopic total gastrectomy procedure.
The surgeon begins a clockwise dissection around the stomach, likely focusing on mobilizing structures or accessing specific anatomical areas as part of the gastrectomy procedure.
The surgeon performs a lymphadenectomy at station six, carefully excising lymphatic tissue from the specified anatomical area.
The surgeon uses an ultrasonic device to perform dissection, focusing on precise tissue separation and minimizing bleeding.
The surgical field reveals a significant amount of adipose tissue in the current region being dissected.
The surgeon searches for the gastric block vessels, navigating through the surrounding tissue using the ultrasonic device.
The surgeon dissects the gastric epiploid vein, carefully separating it from surrounding tissues using precise techniques.
Usually that's the most difficult part of this procedure.
In the obese patients it's much more difficult to do this step.
The surgeon clips and divides the gastric epiploid vein, separating it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon dissects the gastric epipelagic artery, carefully separating it from surrounding tissues.
The surgical field reveals proximity to the pancreas head, with surrounding tissues visible.
The surgeon positions underneath the duodenum to dissect the gastroduodenal artery, carefully separating it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon halts the dissection and places a gauze underneath the duodenum before proceeding with the dissection of the hepatic hilum.
The surgeon performs a lymphadenectomy at the XII-A station, carefully excising lymphatic tissue from the surrounding area.
The surgeon isolates and divides the right gastric artery, completing this step in the gastrectomy procedure.
The surgeon performs a lymphadenectomy at Station V, carefully dissecting and removing lymphatic tissue near the pancreas head.
The surgeon performs a lymphadenectomy at the Eight-A station, carefully excising lymphatic tissue in the specified area.
The surgical team advances to stations seven and nine without performing dissection in these areas.
The surgeon carefully dissects the left gastric artery, isolating it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon clips and divides the vessel, followed by performing a lymphadenectomy at station one. The lymph nodes at station two are removed, and the procedure progresses to station eleven D.
The surgeon divides the short gastric vessels and performs a level IV-SA lymphadenectomy, carefully dissecting and removing lymphatic tissue from the specified anatomical station.
The surgeon staples the duodenum using a blue cartridge stapler, securing the tissue in preparation for further surgical steps.
The surgeon staples the esophagus using an articulated vise, securing the tissue in preparation for further surgical steps.
The surgeon performs a Roux-en-Y reconstruction, visually connecting the jejunum to the esophagus and duodenum, creating a Y-shaped configuration for gastric bypass.
The surgeon uses the Orville device to perform the anastomosis, connecting the esophagus and the reconstructed gastric pouch.
The surgeon performs an esophagogastric anastomosis using a 21mm circular stapler, connecting the esophagus to the gastric remnant.
The surgeon reinforces the staple line using a 3-0 suture, securing the area with precise stitching along the previously stapled tissue.
The surgeon fixes the jejunal segment to the crossbar, securing it in place as part of the Roux-en-Y reconstruction.
The surgeon performs entero-anastomosis using a stapler, followed by closure of mesenteric defects. The adenostomy is completed as part of the procedure.
The extracted specimen is visible after the extension of the optical port.
Postoperative period was uneventful.
